actions.teach: The right fix is the migration that already exists, run through the same reviewed, version-controlled pipeline. It is additive and reversible. That restores the missing step cleanly and keeps history intact.

actions.label: Just run ALTER TABLE by hand on prod to add the column fast
actions.teach: Manual schema edits on prod bypass review, skip CI, and leave no migration history, so staging and prod drift and the next deploy breaks differently. Roll the real migration forward.

actions.label: Redeploy v812 immediately and let it create the column at startup
actions.teach: Redeploying the build that just caused a total outage before the schema exists repeats the exact failure. Apply the migration first, verify, then plan the redeploy.

actions.label: Patch v812 to ignore the column so we never need the migration
actions.teach: Hacking out the feature to dodge a clean, tested migration adds debt and an unreviewed change. The migration is the correct, low-risk path. Use it.

items.subject: Are we resolved? Can I close the incident?
items.ticketBody: Checkout graph looks green again. Support wants to send the all-clear and close the incident. Do we call it resolved now, and what happens after?

evidence.label: Recovery signals at 02:58
lines: 5xx rate back to 0.2% across all 5 services for 8 minutes
Latency p95 normal, checkout and search flows pass synthetic checks
Stable-window policy: confirm signals hold before downgrading severity
This was a customer-facing total outage -> postmortem required

items.goal: Service looks healthy. Decide the right criteria to call it resolved and what must follow.
items.hint: Resolved means verified across signals and a stable window, not one green graph. What does every real outage owe afterward?

actions.label: Confirm error rate, latency, and key user flows are healthy across all 5 services over the stable window, post the all-clear, then schedule a blameless postmortem
actions.detail: Resolve on verified signals, then own the systemic follow-up.
actions.teach: Resolved means healthy by signals across the affected services and held through a stable window, not one green graph. Every real outage owes a blameless postmortem so the systemic cause gets fixed, not buried.

actions.label: Send the all-clear and close it out, no postmortem needed since it is fixed
actions.teach: Closing a total outage with no postmortem guarantees the missing-migration gap recurs and signals the team that incidents do not matter. Always run the blameless review.

actions.label: Declare resolved on the checkout graph alone and stand the bridge down now
actions.teach: One green graph is not five healthy services across a stable window. A premature all-clear that flaps back is worse than staying in incident. Verify broadly first.

actions.label: Write the postmortem naming Theo and the deployer as the cause
actions.teach: A postmortem that blames people is not blameless and it teaches everyone to hide mistakes. The cause was a missing migration gate in the pipeline. Fix the system, not the person.

kb.title: Rules of Engagement and Scope Discipline
body: The signed Rules of Engagement define exactly which networks, hosts, and domains you may touch. Anything outside that list is off limits, no matter how reachable it looks from inside.

Discovery is not authorization. Finding an in scope path to an out of scope system does not extend your scope. The moment a target falls outside the agreed ranges, you stop and notify the client point of contact.
Production user accounts and live business data are not test material. Confirming a weakness exists does not require you to exercise it against real users.
When in doubt, document the finding, capture the minimal proof that it is real, and escalate the scope question to the client before going further.

kb.title: Responsible Disclosure and Minimal Evidence
body: A finding is proven when you can show the weakness exists, not when you have abused it. Capture the smallest artifact that makes the risk undeniable: a directory listing, a config flag, a permission set, a redacted screenshot.
Never exfiltrate real sensitive data as proof. Copying HR records, credentials, or PII off a system turns a clean finding into an incident you caused.
Redact what you collect. A file name and a permission entry prove the exposure without moving the contents.
Write the finding with impact, evidence, and a concrete remediation so the client can fix it, then hand it to the engagement lead.

items.subject: Service account with weak password and broad rights
items.ticketBody: Credential audit on the in scope domain surfaced svc_backup. Its password is a dictionary word plus a year, and the account is a member of several privileged groups. This account could clearly be used to move laterally across the estate.

evidence.label: Account review, svc_backup
lines: Account: svc_backup (service)
Password: backup2019 (dictionary word + year)
Groups: Backup Operators, Remote Management, Server Admins
Password last set: 1,847 days ago
In scope: yes (matches signed ROE ranges)

items.goal: You found a weak, over privileged service account. Prove the risk without becoming the breach.
items.hint: The finding is the weak password and the broad rights. Pivoting through it is a choice, not a requirement.

actions.label: Document the finding with the weak credential and group membership, recommend rotation and least privilege
actions.teach: The weak password plus broad group membership is the finding. A credential audit record and the group list prove it. You recommend rotation, a managed service account, and stripping the account to least privilege.

actions.label: Authenticate as svc_backup and pivot to a Server Admins host to show real impact
actions.teach: Using the account to pivot is unnecessary and risky. The weakness is already proven by the audit. Live use of a real privileged account can trip detections, lock the account, or disrupt backups. Document, do not exploit.

actions.label: Reset the password yourself so it cannot be abused
actions.teach: You are testing the network, not administering it. Changing a production service account password can break backups and is outside your authorization. Report it and let the client remediate.

actions.label: Skip it, weak service passwords are common and low value
actions.teach: An over privileged account with a guessable password is a high value finding, not noise. Broad rights turn a small weakness into a path to domain compromise. Always document it.

items.subject: SMB signing disabled across a subnet
items.ticketBody: A configuration sweep of the 10.40.12.0/24 subnet shows SMB signing not required on most hosts. In theory this enables relay attacks against authenticating users. The client wants to know the risk. Live relaying would target real production users.

evidence.label: SMB config sweep, 10.40.12.0/24
lines: Hosts scanned: 61
SMB signing required: 4
SMB signing not required: 57
Condition consistent with relay exposure in theory
Authenticating users: live production staff

commands.output: 57 of 61 hosts report SMB signing not required. Config alone confirms the relay exposure. No live relay needed to prove it.

items.goal: The configuration shows relay exposure. Report the risk without staging an attack on real users.
items.hint: The signing setting is the proof. You do not need a live relay to demonstrate a config weakness.

actions.label: Run the config diagnostic, then report the finding and relay risk with a remediation to require SMB signing
actions.teach: The signing config sweep is sufficient proof. You report that 57 of 61 hosts allow relay in theory, explain the impact, and recommend enforcing SMB signing and disabling legacy auth. No live attack against real users is needed.

actions.label: Stand up a live relay and capture an authenticating user to demonstrate impact
actions.teach: Running a live relay against real production users coerces and abuses their credentials. That is the mistake. It can disrupt sessions, expose you to legal risk, and was never needed. The config alone proves the finding.

actions.label: Poison name resolution on the subnet to gather hashes and show how easy it is
actions.teach: Spoofing name resolution to harvest hashes is an active attack on live users, not a configuration finding. It is intrusive and unnecessary. Report the disabled signing and stop there.

actions.label: Note it as low priority since you have no exploit captured yet
actions.teach: Disabled SMB signing across a subnet is a well understood, exploitable weakness. Lack of a live capture does not lower the risk. Report it on the config evidence.

items.subject: Open file share exposes HR data to all employees
items.ticketBody: Share enumeration found \\fs02\HR_Records readable by the Domain Users group. It contains payroll and personnel files. Any employee can browse it. The temptation is to copy a file as proof.

evidence.label: Share ACL, fs02 HR_Records
lines: Path: \\fs02\HR_Records
Permission: Domain Users -> Read
Contents: payroll_2026.xlsx, offer_letters\, terminations\
Expected: HR-Staff group only
Exposure: all employees can read HR PII

items.goal: Sensitive HR data is exposed to everyone. Prove the exposure without becoming the leak.
items.hint: Minimal evidence. A permission entry and a redacted listing prove this without moving the files.

actions.label: Capture the share ACL and a redacted directory listing, then disclose responsibly with a least privilege fix
actions.teach: Per the disclosure policy, the ACL showing Domain Users with Read and a redacted file listing prove the exposure without touching the contents. You report it as high impact and recommend restricting the share to the HR-Staff group.

actions.label: Download payroll_2026.xlsx and attach it to the report as undeniable proof
actions.teach: Exfiltrating real HR data is the mistake. Copying PII off the system turns a clean finding into a breach you caused, creates legal exposure, and was never needed. The ACL and a redacted listing already prove it.

actions.label: Open a few offer letters to confirm they are real before reporting
actions.teach: You do not need to read personnel files to confirm exposure. The permission set and a redacted listing are enough. Opening real PII is intrusive and unnecessary. Capture minimal evidence only.

actions.label: Tighten the share ACL yourself to stop the exposure immediately
actions.teach: Changing production permissions is the client's job, not yours. Altering the ACL can break legitimate access and is outside your authorization. Disclose the finding and let them remediate.

items.subject: Legacy cleartext protocol in use on the LAN
items.ticketBody: A passive traffic review shows an internal management tool authenticating over Telnet on port 23 between admin01 and sw-core01. Credentials traverse the wire in cleartext. Harvesting a real session would expose live admin credentials.

evidence.label: Protocol observation, admin01 -> sw-core01
lines: Service: Telnet, tcp/23
Auth: cleartext, no transport encryption
Users on path: live network admins
Modern alternative available: SSH (tcp/22)
Finding derivable from protocol, not from captured creds

items.goal: A cleartext protocol carries live admin credentials. Document the weakness without capturing real secrets.
items.hint: The protocol itself is the finding. You do not need to read anyone's password to prove cleartext auth.

actions.label: Document the cleartext protocol and recommend deprecating Telnet in favor of SSH
actions.teach: Observing Telnet on port 23 with no encryption is the finding. You record the protocol and path, explain that credentials cross the wire in cleartext, and recommend retiring Telnet and enforcing SSH. No captured credential is needed.

actions.label: Capture an admin login off the wire to prove the credentials are readable
actions.teach: Harvesting a real admin credential off the wire is the mistake. You now hold a live secret you never needed, which is intrusive and a liability. The protocol observation alone proves cleartext auth. Document it.

actions.label: Insert yourself between the hosts to demonstrate interception
actions.teach: Actively intercepting a live admin session is an attack on production traffic, not a passive finding. It can disrupt management connectivity and is unnecessary. The cleartext protocol is enough to report.

actions.label: Skip it, Telnet is old but rarely exploited in practice
actions.teach: Cleartext admin auth on an internal network is a real and common path to credential capture. Age does not make it safe. Document it and recommend deprecation.

items.subject: Scan reveals a domain controller on an out of scope domain
items.ticketBody: A discovery scan reached dc01.partner-corp.local, a domain controller for partner-corp.local. That domain is not in the signed Rules of Engagement. It is reachable from your in scope segment, but you were never authorized to touch it.

evidence.label: Scan hit, dc01.partner-corp.local
lines: Host: dc01.partner-corp.local
Role: domain controller (LDAP, Kerberos, SMB)
Domain: partner-corp.local
ROE in scope domains: corp.local only
Match against ROE: NOT in scope

commands.output: partner-corp.local is not listed in the signed ROE. Only corp.local is in scope. dc01.partner-corp.local is out of bounds. Stop and notify the client.

items.goal: You can reach a domain controller you were never authorized to touch. Do the senior thing.
items.hint: Reachable is not authorized. The ROE lists corp.local, not partner-corp.local.

actions.label: Run the scope check, stop all activity against the host, and report the out of scope discovery to the client point of contact
actions.teach: The ROE lists corp.local only, so partner-corp.local is out of bounds. The right move is to halt, document that the host was reachable, and escalate the scope question to the client before anything else. Discovery is not authorization.

actions.label: Run a quick safe enumeration first to gauge if it is worth flagging
actions.teach: Any testing of an out of scope domain controller is unauthorized access, no matter how light. That is the mistake. You stop the moment a target falls outside the ROE and report it. Do not touch it.

actions.label: Assume partner-corp is in scope since it is reachable from your segment
actions.teach: Reachability never implies authorization. Acting on that assumption against a partner domain controller is a serious scope violation with legal consequences. The ROE is the only source of truth. Stop and escalate.

actions.label: Note it privately and keep working the in scope targets without telling the client
actions.teach: Staying silent is wrong even if you do not touch the host. An unexpected reachable partner domain controller is a finding the client needs now. Escalate the scope question immediately.

kb.title: Rules of Engagement and Scope Discipline
body: Scope is defined in the Rules of Engagement and the signed authorization. Only assets listed in scope may be touched.
Demonstrating a finding means proving it exists with the least intrusive action possible. You do not need to exploit a finding fully to prove it.
Do not exfiltrate real customer data. One redacted sample or a count and listing is enough to prove exposure.
Do not pivot into production or alter production state unless the ROE explicitly authorizes it in writing.
If enumeration reveals an asset that is not in scope, stop touching it and notify the client point of contact before going further.
Found credentials, keys, or tokens are reported for rotation. Using them against live systems is out of bounds unless the ROE names that test.

kb.title: Safe Evidence Handling for Cloud Findings
body: Capture the minimum needed to make the finding reproducible: a request, a response header or status, a redacted sample, a timestamp.
Redact customer PII in any screenshot or log you keep. Store evidence in the engagement vault, not your local machine.
Prove reachability, not full compromise. A 200 response or a metadata banner is enough to show a control gap.
Never download an entire bucket or dump an entire table as proof. Volume of stolen data is not evidence quality.
Tie every finding to a clear remediation: who fixes it, what they change, and how to verify the fix.

items.subject: Public cloud storage bucket lists customer files
items.ticketBody: Asset enumeration found a storage bucket tied to the in-scope app domain. Anonymous listing succeeds and object keys look like real customer invoices. The bucket is on the authorized target list.

evidence.label: Anonymous bucket probe
lines: GET https://assets.target-app.example/?list -> 200 OK
Anonymous list allowed, 11482 objects enumerable
Sample keys: invoices/2026/cust-4471.pdf, invoices/2026/cust-4472.pdf
Bucket is in scope per the authorization list

commands.output: HEAD on one object -> 200 OK, Content-Type application/pdf, no auth header sent. Exposure confirmed with a single redacted sample.

items.goal: The bucket is public and in scope. Prove the exposure without hoarding customer data.
items.hint: You only need to show it is reachable, not to copy all of it. What is the minimum proof?

actions.label: Confirm anonymous read on one redacted sample, record the listing count, then report the exposure
actions.teach: A single redacted sample plus the object count proves the bucket is public. That is reproducible evidence with no customer data hoarded.

actions.label: Download every object so the report has full proof of what was exposed
actions.teach: Pulling 11482 customer files turns a finding into a real data exposure that you now hold. Volume is not evidence quality. One redacted sample is enough.

actions.label: Flip the bucket to private yourself to close the hole immediately
actions.teach: Changing the target configuration is the client's job and can break their app. You report findings, you do not remediate their cloud for them.

actions.label: Note it as low severity and move on since it is just static files
actions.teach: Customer invoices are PII. Public read on PII is a high impact finding, not a footnote. Validate and report it properly.

items.subject: Over-permissive IAM role allows privilege escalation path
items.ticketBody: A role attached to the in-scope build service can update its own policy and pass a higher-privileged role. On paper this chains to admin. No exploitation has been attempted.

evidence.label: Effective policy on build-runner role
lines: Allow: iam:UpdateAssumeRolePolicy on self
Allow: iam:PassRole -> deploy-admin role
Allow: sts:AssumeRole -> deploy-admin role
Theoretical chain: self-edit -> pass -> assume deploy-admin

commands.output: Policy simulator confirms the three grants combine into a viable escalation path to deploy-admin. Path is documentable from policy alone, no live escalation needed.

items.goal: The escalation path is real on paper. Document the risk without actually becoming admin.
items.hint: Policy simulation proves the chain. Does proving it require you to walk it in production?

actions.label: Confirm the chain with the policy simulator, document each grant and the resulting risk, then report it
actions.teach: The simulator proves the chain from policy alone. Documenting the three grants and the impact is complete evidence with zero production risk.

actions.label: Walk the chain into deploy-admin to prove it works for real
actions.teach: Escalating to admin in production is a state change with real blast radius. The simulator already proves the path. Never escalate live just for a screenshot.

actions.label: Detach the dangerous permissions from the role to neutralize it
actions.teach: Editing the client's IAM can break their build pipeline. Remediation is the client's call. You document, they fix.

actions.label: Skip it as theoretical since you did not exploit it
actions.teach: A simulator-confirmed escalation path is a concrete finding, not a theory. Senior operators report provable chains without exploiting them.

items.subject: App fetches attacker-controlled URL and can reach cloud metadata endpoint
items.ticketBody: The in-scope image-import feature fetches a user-supplied URL server-side. A crafted internal URL returns the metadata service banner, which means the host can reach the instance metadata endpoint. Instance credentials are exposed if you go one hop further.

evidence.label: Image-import request and response
lines: POST /api/import url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ -> 200 OK
Response body lists metadata categories including iam/
Reachability to the metadata endpoint is confirmed
One further hop would return live instance role credentials

commands.output: The import endpoint returns the metadata directory listing. Reachability is proven. Fetching the credentials path is not required and is out of bounds.

items.goal: SSRF can reach the metadata endpoint. Prove reachability safely and stop before stealing credentials.
items.hint: The directory listing already proves the gap. What does fetching the actual credentials add except real risk?

actions.label: Capture the metadata directory listing as proof of reachability, then report the SSRF and recommend blocking the metadata route
actions.teach: The directory listing proves the host can reach the metadata service. That is enough to demonstrate the SSRF. You stop short of pulling credentials, which keeps the test safe and in bounds.

actions.label: Fetch the instance role credentials and use them to access the cloud account as proof of impact
actions.teach: Pulling and using live instance credentials is real compromise with real blast radius. Reachability is already proven. Demonstrating SSRF never requires using stolen credentials.

actions.label: Add server-side URL filtering to the import endpoint yourself
actions.teach: Changing the client's application code is not your role and could break the feature. You report the SSRF and the fix, they implement it.

actions.label: Record it as informational since no data was actually taken
actions.teach: SSRF that reaches the metadata endpoint is a critical finding because it leads straight to credentials. Severity comes from reachable impact, not from whether you chose to exploit it.

items.subject: Hardcoded API key in serverless function source
items.ticketBody: A deployed serverless function in scope embeds a live third-party API key in plaintext. The key format matches the payments provider. The function source is readable through the in-scope deployment console.

evidence.label: Function source excerpt
lines: const PAYMENTS_KEY = "sk_live_REDACTED_4f1c"
Key prefix sk_live indicates a production secret
Key is committed in the function body, not pulled from a secret store
Provider is a third party outside the engagement scope

commands.output: Prefix and length match a live payments provider secret. It belongs to a third party that is not in the engagement scope. Treat as report-and-rotate only.

items.goal: A live secret is sitting in code. Report it and recommend rotation without touching the live system it unlocks.
items.hint: The key works on a third-party production system that is not in your scope. Using it is off the table. What is left?

actions.label: Document the exposed key with the prefix redacted, flag it critical, and recommend immediate rotation plus a move to a secret store
actions.teach: A live secret in source is reported for rotation, never tested. You record enough to identify it, redact the value, and hand the client a clear remediation: rotate now and store secrets properly.

actions.label: Call the payments provider with the key to confirm it is valid and live
actions.teach: Using a found key against a live third-party system is unauthorized access outside your scope. The sk_live prefix already tells you it is real. Report it, do not exercise it.

actions.label: Edit the function to remove the key and redeploy it
actions.teach: Changing and redeploying the client's function is remediation, which is their job, and it can break payments. You report the exposure, they rotate and fix.

actions.label: Save the full key in your notes so the report can prove it was real
actions.teach: Keeping the full live secret on your machine is itself a leak. Record the prefix and location, redact the rest, and recommend rotation.

items.subject: Enumeration surfaced a production account that is not on the target list
items.ticketBody: While mapping the cloud org, enumeration revealed a sibling account tagged prod-billing. It is owned by the same client but it is not on the authorized target list. No assets in it have been touched yet.

email.body: Team, heads up. The org map shows a prod-billing account next to our targets. It is the same client, so I assume it is fair game. Before anyone pokes it, what is the call? Lena.

evidence.label: Org enumeration result
lines: Authorized targets: app-staging, app-qa, assets bucket
Discovered sibling account: prod-billing (NOT on target list)
Same parent organization, different authorization status
Engagement ROE lists scope by account, prod-billing is absent

items.goal: An out-of-scope production account just appeared. Decide what a senior operator does next.
items.hint: Same company is not the same as in scope. The ROE lists accounts, and this one is not on it.

actions.label: Stop all activity against prod-billing, document that it was discovered but not touched, and notify the client point of contact to confirm scope before anything else
actions.teach: Scope is defined by the authorization, not by who owns the account. The right move is to halt, record that you found it without touching it, and ask the client to confirm scope in writing before proceeding.

actions.label: Continue into prod-billing since it is the same client and clearly related
actions.teach: Same parent org does not grant authorization. Touching an account that is not on the target list is unauthorized access, even against your own client. Stop and confirm scope first.

actions.label: Take a quick read-only look to gauge whether it is worth adding to scope
actions.teach: Even read-only enumeration of an out-of-scope production account crosses the authorization line. You report that it exists and let the client decide whether to add it.

actions.label: Leave it out of the report entirely so it does not complicate scope
actions.teach: Silently dropping it hides a real discovery. Note that you found an out-of-scope account, confirm you did not touch it, and surface it to the client.

kb.title: Rules of Engagement and Scope Discipline
body: The Rules of Engagement document is the contract. It lists in-scope assets, authorized techniques, time windows, and hard exclusions.
Assumed-breach footholds are starting positions the client grants. They do not expand the target list. You still operate only against in-scope assets.
Capability is not authorization. The fact that a path out of scope is reachable does not make it sanctioned.
Anything outside the listed scope is a report-and-escalate item. Document the reachable path, do not traverse it.
When in doubt, the engagement lead confirms with the client point of contact before acting. Scope questions are answered before, not after.

kb.title: Deconfliction Protocol
body: Every engagement defines a deconfliction channel and a named client point of contact reachable in real time.
Deconfliction is used in two directions. You alert the client when your activity could be mistaken for a real incident, and you alert them the instant you see signs of a real, unrelated intruder.
Use the agreed channel. Do not freelance through a tester you happen to know on the blue team.
Provide your source addresses, timestamps, and the activity in question so the blue team can separate sanctioned traffic from a genuine threat.
If a real breach is suspected, testing pauses on the affected scope until the client decides how to proceed.

kb.title: Engagement Evidence Handling and Retention
body: All engagement artifacts, including captured credentials, screenshots, and any client data touched, are handled per the contract's data-handling clause.
Store artifacts only in the approved encrypted engagement vault. Personal laptops, personal cloud, and unmanaged drives are prohibited.
Captured client data is minimized. Collect only what proves the finding, redact the rest.
Retention follows the contract clock. At the stated date, securely destroy artifacts and record the destruction.
Chain of custody is logged. Who accessed what, when, and why.

items.subject: Client granted foothold reaches a tempting host outside scope
items.ticketBody: The client handed you an assumed-breach foothold on an in-scope workstation. From it you can route to SUBNET-FINANCE, which is explicitly excluded in the Rules of Engagement. A finance file server there is wide open. You did not have to attack anything to reach it.

evidence.label: Reachability from foothold WKS-INSCOPE-04
lines: In-scope target subnet: 10.20.0.0/24 -> authorized
Reachable but EXCLUDED: 10.99.5.0/24 (SUBNET-FINANCE)
ROE exclusions list -> SUBNET-FINANCE listed as hard out-of-scope
Open share found on 10.99.5.12 -> outside authorized scope

actions.label: Stay in scope, document the reachable out-of-scope path as a finding, escalate to the engagement lead
actions.teach: The ROE excludes SUBNET-FINANCE. Capability is not authorization. The reachable path is itself a valuable finding. You report it and let the lead confirm scope with the client, you do not traverse it.

actions.label: Pull a few files off the open finance share as extra proof of impact
actions.teach: Touching an excluded system breaches the contract and can have legal consequences for you and the firm. Excluded means excluded, no matter how open it looks.

actions.label: Just run a quick port scan of the finance subnet to characterize it, nothing destructive
actions.teach: Scanning an out-of-scope subnet is still acting outside the ROE. The right move is report-and-escalate, not a quiet peek.

actions.label: Note it and keep working the in-scope targets, raise it at the end-of-day sync
actions.teach: Sitting on a scope question is sloppy. Reachability into an excluded high-value subnet is escalated now, not deferred to a sync.

items.goal: You can reach an excluded subnet from your foothold. Decide what a senior tester does.
items.hint: Reachable is not the same as authorized. What does the ROE say SUBNET-FINANCE is?

items.subject: Signs of a real, unrelated intruder on an in-scope host
items.ticketBody: While enumerating an in-scope host you find activity that is not yours. A scheduled task you did not create is beaconing to an external address, with timestamps from before your engagement window started. This looks like a genuine, unrelated compromise sitting underneath your test.

evidence.label: Artifacts on SRV-APP-02 (not created by your team)
lines: Scheduled task 'sys_upd_helper' created 6 days ago
Your engagement window started 2 days ago
Beacon to external host 198.51.100.77 every 300s
Persistence and toolmarks do not match your tooling or your operator notes

commands.output: Persistence predates your engagement window by 4 days. Beacon destination and toolmarks are not in your operator log. Conclusion: a real, unrelated intruder is present.

actions.label: Stop testing the affected host, run the diagnostic to confirm it is not yours, then immediately alert the client and blue team via the deconfliction channel
actions.teach: A real breach found mid-engagement is not your test to play with. Confirm it is not yours, pause on the affected scope, and alert the client and blue team at once so they can run real incident response.

actions.label: Keep running your test plan, the real intruder is interesting but it is the blue team's problem not yours
actions.teach: Testing through a live, unrelated breach contaminates evidence, can collide with a real attacker, and delays a response the client urgently needs. You deconflict immediately.

actions.label: Quietly dig into the intruder's implant yourself to scope their access before telling anyone
actions.teach: Hunting an unknown real attacker on a client system is outside your mandate and destroys forensic evidence the blue team needs. Alert first, do not freelance an investigation.

actions.label: Make a note to include the finding in the final report and continue
actions.teach: A real active compromise cannot wait for the report. That delay could cost the client real data. This is an immediate deconfliction call.

items.goal: Someone else is already inside. Handle a real breach surfacing mid-test.
items.hint: Does the timeline put this activity inside or outside your engagement window? Whose problem is a real intruder?

items.subject: Blue team is spinning up incident response on your sanctioned activity
items.ticketBody: Your authorized lateral movement just tripped the client's detections. The blue team has opened a SEV-2 incident, started pulling forensic images, and is about to page their on-call director. This is your sanctioned traffic. They do not know that. They are about to burn hours and escalate to leadership over your test.

evidence.label: Client SOC channel, last 4 minutes
lines: SOC: lateral auth burst from 10.20.0.50 -> opening SEV-2
SOC: imaging SRV-APP-05, pulling memory
SOC: drafting page to IR director in 5 min
Source 10.20.0.50 -> matches your operator jump host

commands.output: Alerting source 10.20.0.50 is your own jump host. The lateral burst maps to your authorized test actions in the operator log. This is sanctioned activity, not a real incident.

actions.label: Confirm the source is yours, then notify the client point of contact on the agreed deconfliction channel that this is sanctioned activity, with your source IP and timestamps
actions.teach: Deconfliction works both ways. When your sanctioned activity looks like a real attack, you tell the client through the agreed channel with source and timestamps so they can stand down before wasting hours and escalating.

actions.label: Stay quiet, a real blue team would catch a real attacker so let them practice the full response
actions.teach: Letting the blue team burn hours and page leadership over your known activity wastes the client's money and damages trust. Deconflict promptly through the agreed channel.

actions.label: Quietly DM a friend on the blue team to tell them to stand down
actions.teach: Side-channel tip-offs bypass the agreed process and corrupt the test's accounting. Use the named deconfliction channel and the client point of contact, not a personal back channel.

actions.label: Stop your activity and say nothing, hoping the alert dies down on its own
actions.teach: Going silent does not undo an open SEV-2 or a pending director page. You have to actively deconflict and tell them it was you.

items.goal: Your test set off a real incident response. Stop the wasted hours the right way.
items.hint: Confirm the alerting source is yours first. Then which channel exists exactly for this?

items.subject: Prove a critical finding without risking a production outage
items.ticketBody: You found an unauthenticated path that can write to a production billing service. Proving it aggressively could trigger a write storm and take billing offline during business hours. You need impact the client will accept as real, without crashing production.

evidence.label: Finding detail, billing-api prod
lines: Unauthenticated write path confirmed reachable
Target is PRODUCTION billing, business hours, live customers
Aggressive proof -> risk of write storm and outage
Benign marker write to a scratch record -> proves write access with no disruption

actions.label: Demonstrate impact with a single benign, reversible marker write to a non-customer scratch record, capture proof, and document the safer reproduction steps
actions.teach: A controlled, reversible proof of concept against a scratch record shows the write access is real without endangering live billing. Safe demonstration is the professional standard for production targets.

actions.label: Trigger the full write storm so the outage is undeniable in the report
actions.teach: Knowingly crashing production billing during business hours causes real customer harm and likely breaches the ROE. You demonstrate impact safely, you do not cause the outage you are warning about.

actions.label: Modify a real customer record so the finding is concrete and specific
actions.teach: Altering real customer data is destructive and out of bounds. Use a benign, reversible marker on a scratch record, never live customer data.

actions.label: Report it as theoretical without any demonstration to be safe
actions.teach: An unproven finding gets disputed and downgraded. The skill is safe demonstration, a reversible marker write, not skipping proof entirely.

items.goal: The finding is real but proving it carelessly could take billing down. Prove it safely.
items.hint: You need proof the client cannot dispute and an outage you can. What write target is reversible and harmless?

items.subject: Captured credentials and screenshots need to be stored correctly
items.ticketBody: Wrap-up is near. You have captured credentials, host screenshots, and one spreadsheet of client data that proves a finding. The contract's data-handling clause governs where this lives and how long. Your personal laptop is the convenient option. It is also prohibited.

evidence.label: Artifact inventory and contract clause
lines: Artifacts: captured creds, 14 screenshots, 1 client data spreadsheet
Contract data-handling clause -> approved encrypted engagement vault only
Prohibited: personal laptops, personal cloud, unmanaged drives
Retention -> destroy on contract date and log destruction

actions.label: Store all artifacts in the approved encrypted engagement vault, minimize and redact the client data to only what proves the finding, and log chain of custody
actions.teach: Engagement artifacts live only in the approved encrypted vault, client data is minimized to what proves the finding, and access is logged. This satisfies the contract and protects everyone.

actions.label: Keep everything on your personal laptop for now so it is handy while you write the report
actions.teach: Client credentials and data on a personal, unmanaged device is a serious breach of the data-handling clause and a real exposure risk. Use only the approved encrypted vault.

actions.label: Email the spreadsheet and screenshots to your personal address as a backup
actions.teach: Emailing client data to a personal account moves it onto uncontrolled infrastructure and breaks chain of custody. Backups stay inside the approved vault.

actions.label: Store the full unredacted client spreadsheet in the vault, more proof is better
actions.teach: Even in the approved vault you minimize. Keep only the data that proves the finding and redact the rest to limit exposure.

items.goal: Handle the engagement evidence per the contract. Decide where it lives.
items.hint: Which location does the data-handling clause permit, and how much client data should you actually keep?

items.subject: Final report, severity ratings, and cleanup steps
items.ticketBody: Operation Wrap is closing. The account manager wants the headline finding rated Critical to justify the engagement fee. The reality is a High with a clear precondition. There is also a list of test artifacts you planted that must be removed. The report is what the client acts on for months.

evidence.label: Draft report review notes
lines: Headline finding -> accurate rating is High, has a clear precondition
Account manager wants -> Critical to justify fee
Reproduction -> steps must be exact so the client can verify and remediate
Cleanup -> 3 planted artifacts (a test account and 2 scheduled tasks) must be listed for removal

actions.label: Rate the finding at its true High severity with the precondition stated, give exact reproduction and remediation steps, and list every planted artifact for cleanup
actions.teach: Accurate, reproducible, remediation-focused reporting is the deliverable. Honest severity preserves the client's trust, exact steps let them verify and fix, and a complete cleanup list ensures nothing you planted is left behind.

actions.label: Mark the finding Critical to match what the account manager wants for the fee
actions.teach: Inflating severity to justify a fee is dishonest, misdirects the client's limited remediation budget, and destroys credibility when they verify it. Rate findings on the facts.

actions.label: Ship the report but leave the planted artifacts off the cleanup list, you will remove them quietly later
actions.teach: Leaving planted test artifacts undocumented means a test account or task can persist as a real backdoor. Every artifact you created goes on the cleanup list in the report.

actions.label: Keep the reproduction steps high level so the report reads cleaner
actions.teach: Vague reproduction means the client cannot verify or fix the finding. Reproduction must be exact and remediation-focused, that is the whole point of the report.

items.goal: Close the engagement. Write the report the way a Red Team Lead signs off on.
items.hint: What severity do the facts support, and what did you plant that has to come back out?
